input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is the MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, specifically its first axis driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor and gearbox, operating in a machining domain with key characteristics including joint friction.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Services include live-data linking for real-time synchronization between physical and digital entities, dashboard visualization for operational monitoring, and friction identification algorithms to optimize motion control processes.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML for system structure and behavior modeling, Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives, MathWorks Simulink for detailed simulation, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for behavior description. These tools address multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, technical layers) and integrate heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin maintains real-time synchronization with the physical system through continuous ingestion of live sensor data streams, capturing time-series data at high temporal resolution to ensure an accurate and up-to-date digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system employs a Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM) architecture, integrating DataTrace, Processing, and Model components. It uses SysML for structural modeling and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized interoperability, enabling coordinated resource management and seamless integration of heterogeneous system elements.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems utilizes Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized data exchange and MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming. The system interfaces with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl robot controller to facilitate real-time control and data synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs analytical (white-box) friction models derived from physics-based equations and data-driven (black-box) models trained on operational data, integrated within executable simulation models implemented using MathWorks MATLAB Simulink. Data components include contextual data traces and data points aggregated from physical system sensors, managed through structured pipelines for real-time synchronization and historical analysis. Model relationships are defined via SysML and ADLs (ACME, AADL) across multiple abstraction levels to ensure consistency in system representation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure consists of two position encoders per drive unit: a motor encoder mounted directly on the motor shaft and a secondary encoder positioned behind the gearbox to measure transmission errors. Positional uncertainty is quantified and compensated through real-time error correction algorithms, with data transmitted via deterministic industrial protocols for continuous feedback.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data from the physical system is transmitted via MQTT protocol. No details on transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms are specified in the document beyond MQTT streaming.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for data transmission between physical and digital components. No details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability mechanisms, or security protocols are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle stages involve Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) during the development phase, where modeling is split into distinct levels of abstraction: requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are used to manage complexity across these stages.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), employing methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs model components, interfaces, and interconnections, while behavior is described via MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation and code generation in C++ or Java.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system generates insights through joint friction identification, visualizes data via a dashboard interface, and applies processed information to improve robot control accuracy, enabling data-driven decision-making based on real-time analytics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards including SysML [17], AADL [24], ACME [23], MontiArc [25], [26], and integration with MathWorks Simulink [28] for model-based simulation and code generation.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly specific and detailed, accurately reflecting the source text. It correctly identifies the MABI MAX 100 robot, its first axis with specific motor and gearbox components, the machining domain, and the focus on joint friction. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction correctly identifies the key services provided by the digital shadow, including live-data linking, dashboard visualization, and the specific service of friction identification for control optimization. It captures the purpose and capabilities well. Supported by quotes: 'live-data linking', 'visualization in a dashboard', 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction... to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy'.

The extraction accurately lists the tools and methodologies described in the background section of the source document. It correctly identifies MBSE, SysML, specific ADLs (ACME, AADL, etc.), Simulink, and programming languages. Quotes: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'To describe the functional and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used, e.g., ACME, AADL, MontiArc'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The text describes the system as 'real-time, data-driven representations' that integrate 'operational data, and live measurements'. It explicitly states that data consists of 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT', supporting the extraction's claims about continuous ingestion and real-time synchronization. The mention of 'high temporal resolution' is implied by the use of time-series and streaming data, though not explicitly quantified in the text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source text does not contain information about multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination between twins. The paper focuses on a single digital shadow instance for the robot's first axis ('FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow').

The text provides a detailed description of the system's architecture, explicitly defining a 'Digital Shadow Reference Model (DSRM)' that integrates concepts like 'DataTrace', 'Processing', and 'Model'. It confirms the methodology involves 'SysML' for structural modeling and 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' for implementation, matching the specific technical details in the extraction.

The text identifies the specific external hardware, stating 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', and specifies the data transmission method: 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'. It also mentions the implementation of 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' within the methodology, supporting the claims about integration protocols and interoperability standards.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies the use of analytical (white-box) and data-driven (black-box) friction models, the implementation in MathWorks MATLAB Simulink, and the use of SysML and ADLs (ACME, AADL) for defining model relationships. The description of data components aligns perfectly with the digital shadow reference model discussed.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly describes the physical actuators and control system. This includes the permanent magnet synchronous motor, the gearbox components (gear transmission, cycloidal gear), and the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller with specific control capabilities and ranges. Quotes: 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl', 'maximum working range to [-90, 90], and the maximum achievable speed is |q ˙ max | = 75 [◦]/s'.

The extraction is specific and accurate regarding the sensor arrangement, correctly describing the two encoders per drive ('motor encoder is mounted directly on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox'). It also accurately reflects the purpose of this setup to 'reduce backlash and positional uncertainty'. The mention of transmission protocols is slightly less specific than the text ('streamed via MQTT'), but the core technical description is excellent.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source document contains relevant information regarding uncertainty sources and validation methods. It mentions that friction (and thus accuracy) depends on factors like load and temperature (uncertainty sources) and defines the solution scope as the deviation between simulated and measured data (validation). Quotes: 'depends on various factors such as load, temperature, and time', 'solution scope of the deviation of simulated data versus measured data'.
